Mechanism: three stages and their links

Stage 1, language pretraining on unconstrained documents and long conversation threads, the only stage whose loss measures imitation; stage 2, simulation training on an economic market simulator, the main effort; stage 3, real problems as the transfer test, later.

The stage-2 simulator: independent environment states drive dependent stock states; the model holds a position and makes buy/sell decisions from proxies delivered as language reports by the translator LLM from labeled sources of varying reliability (News, Advisor), with reliability a program parameter expressed through rendering instructions. Source labels are in the message format from the first design, before unreliability arrives as curriculum, and they are the foundation for multi-role interaction. Complexity scales through stocks, latent states, coupling, noise, lag, and misinformation.

Links between stages: stage-2 vocabulary is by design a subset of stage-1 vocabulary, ensured by unconstrained diverse generation plus folding stage-2-shaped samples into stage-1 data; stage-1 replay continues through stage 2 (replay_fraction machinery already built); the stage-1 exit threshold is implemented as a stopping mechanism whose measure is decided empirically from pilot runs, with the listener interpretability rate the leading candidate. A standing design constraint on the simulator: the information advantage must be the only edge, meaning price dynamics are constructed so that ignoring the reports has near-zero expected return, otherwise the model learns to trade without reading and stage 2 stops training comprehension. Realized per-step scores reward luck as well as judgment; batch averaging over parallel games absorbs most variance, and expected-value scoring given the provided information is a later dial.
